item.title: Diplomatic efforts seek to avert Trump split with UN refugee agency, sources say
item.description: The Trump administration has in recent weeks weighed cutting ties with ​the U.N. refugee agency, prompting a campaign by diplomats and U.N. officials to persuade the United States to maintain support, sources familiar with the matter ‌told Reuters.
item.content:encoded:
• UNHCR urged supporters to press U.S. officials, sources say
  • Trump candidate critical of UNHCR missed out on No. 2 position
  • US is on board of refugee agency, was its top donor
  • Worries arise about future of UN Refugee Convention

The Reuters Daily Briefing newsletter provides all the news you need to start your day. Sign up here.

Additional reporting by Jonathan Landay and Patricia Zengerle in Washington; Editing by Don Durfee and Sanjeev Miglani

Our Standards: The Thomson Reuters Trust Principles., opens new tab

Emma Farge reports on the U.N. beat and Swiss news from Geneva since 2019. She has produced a string of exclusives on diplomacy, the environment and global trade and covered Switzerland’s first war crimes trial. Her Reuters career started in 2009 covering oil swaps from London and she has since written about the West African Ebola outbreak, embedded with U.N. troops in north Mali and was the first reporter to enter deposed Gambian dictator Yahya Jammeh’s estate. She co-authored a winning story for the Elizabeth Neuffer Memorial Prize on Russia’s diplomatic isolation in 2022 and was also part of a team of journalists nominated in 2012 as Pulitzer finalists in the international reporting category for coverage of the Libyan revolution. She holds a BA from Oxford University (First) and an MSc from the LSE in International Relations. She is currently on the board of the press association for UN correspondents in Geneva (ACANU).

item.title: US Justice Department says UC San Diego's medical school favors Black, Hispanic applicants
item.description: The U.S. Department of ​Justice said on Monday that admissions practices at the University of California San Diego ‌School of Medicine were biased in favor of Black and Hispanic applicants, as the Trump administration continues its crackdown on diversity policies at colleges.
item.content:encoded:
• Trump has cracked down on top schools
  • Trump administration has targeted diversity initiatives
  • Department of Justice made similar assertions about other medical schools this year

Jumpstart your morning with the latest legal news delivered straight to your inbox from The Daily Docket newsletter. Sign up here.

BROADER ⁠CRACKDOWN ON SCHOOLS

Our Standards: The Thomson Reuters Trust Principles., opens new tab

Kanishka Singh is a breaking news reporter for Reuters in Washington DC, who primarily covers US politics and national affairs in his current role. His past breaking news coverage has spanned across a range of topics like the Black Lives Matter movement; the US elections; the 2021 Capitol riots and their follow up probes; the Brexit deal; US-China trade tensions; the NATO withdrawal from Afghanistan; the COVID-19 pandemic; and a 2019 Supreme Court verdict on a religious dispute site in his native India.

item.title: FBI Director Kash Patel planning a trip to Russia, Politico reports
item.description: FBI Director ‌Kash ​Patel ​is planning ⁠a ​trip ​to Russia, likely ​in ​mid-October, Politico ‌reported ⁠on Monday, ​citing ​a ⁠source.
item.content:encoded:
The Reuters Daily Briefing newsletter provides all the news you need to start your day. Sign up here.

Reporting ​by ​Kanishka ⁠Singh in ⁠Washington; ​Editing ​by ​Caitlin Webber

Our Standards: The Thomson Reuters Trust Principles., opens new tab

item.title: US judge approves Anthropic's $1.5 billion settlement of copyright lawsuit
item.description: A federal judge in San Francisco on Monday signed off on ‌artificial intelligence company Anthropic's landmark $1.5 billion settlement of a class action lawsuit brought by a group of authors who accused it of misusing their books to train its AI chatbot Claude.
item.content:encoded:
• $1.5 billion settlement largest known payout in U.S. copyright case
  • Case part of a wave of lawsuits from copyright holders against AI companies
  • Some authors and publishers opted out and continue separate ​cases against Anthropic

The Reuters Daily Briefing newsletter provides all the news you need to start your day. Sign up here.

Our Standards: The Thomson Reuters Trust Principles., opens new tab

Blake Brittain reports on intellectual property law, including patents, trademarks, copyrights and trade secrets, for Reuters Legal. He has previously written for Bloomberg Law and Thomson Reuters Practical Law and practiced as an attorney.

item.title: US DOJ says it is probing Harvard over financial aid programs
item.description: The U.S. Department of Justice said on Monday ‌it launched a probe reviewing whether some Harvard University financial aid programs were discriminatory, marking the latest fight against the university by President Donald Trump's administration.
item.content:encoded:
• Trump has targeted Harvard, other schools with probes
  • Rights advocates have raised concerns
  • Harvard denies engaging in discriminatory policies

Jumpstart your morning with the latest legal news delivered straight to your inbox from The Daily Docket newsletter. Sign up here.

Our Standards: The Thomson Reuters Trust Principles., opens new tab

Kanishka Singh is a breaking news reporter for Reuters in Washington DC, who primarily covers US politics and national affairs in his current role. His past breaking news coverage has spanned across a range of topics like the Black Lives Matter movement; the US elections; the 2021 Capitol riots and their follow up probes; the Brexit deal; US-China trade tensions; the NATO withdrawal from Afghanistan; the COVID-19 pandemic; and a 2019 Supreme Court verdict on a religious dispute site in his native India.

item.title: What has made the cyclospora behind the US outbreak so challenging to trace?
item.description: Cyclospora, the parasite behind thousands of suspected U.S. cases of a foodborne illness, has a long incubation period, and samples cannot be cultured in labs, making it especially ​challenging to trace.
item.content:encoded:
• Tiny parasite caused multistate cyclosporiasis outbreak
  • Cyclosporiasis symptoms can take up to two weeks to appear
  • No whole-genome sequencing can be done with the pathogen

Jumpstart your morning with the latest legal news delivered straight to your inbox from The Daily Docket newsletter. Sign up here.

CYCLOSPORA TESTING IS DIFFICULT

Our Standards: The Thomson Reuters Trust Principles., opens new tab

item.title: Trump says he signed proclamation to adjust tariffs on aluminum imports
item.description: President Donald Trumpsaid on Monday he signed a proclamation ​to adjust tariffs on imports of aluminum into the ‌United States, adding the domestic production of primary aluminum in the U.S. was currently insufficient.
item.content:encoded:
The Reuters Daily Briefing newsletter provides all the news you need to start your day. Sign up here.

  • "In the (Commerce) ​Secretary's view, it is important to modify the ​aluminum tariff regime in a way to more ⁠effectively encourage increased domestic production of primary aluminum," ​Trump said in the proclamation released by the White ​House.
  • U.S. demand for primary aluminum currently outpaces the production capacity of U.S. smelting facilities, according to the proclamation.
  • Trump described aluminum as ​being critical to the U.S. economy and defense ​industrial base.
  • The proclamation directed the U.S. commerce secretary to establish an ‌incentive ⁠program for companies that will invest in building, expanding, or refurbishing aluminum smelters in the United States, the White House said.
  • The program will request onshoring plans ​from companies ​that, if ⁠approved, will be eligible to import a commensurate level of primary aluminum into the ​United States at a reduced tariff rate ​equal ⁠to half of the otherwise applicable Section 232 rate, the White House said in the proclamation.
  • Section 232 of ⁠the ​Trade Expansion Act of 1962 authorizes ​the president to adjust imports found to threaten U.S. national security.

Our Standards: The Thomson Reuters Trust Principles., opens new tab

Kanishka Singh is a breaking news reporter for Reuters in Washington DC, who primarily covers US politics and national affairs in his current role. His past breaking news coverage has spanned across a range of topics like the Black Lives Matter movement; the US elections; the 2021 Capitol riots and their follow up probes; the Brexit deal; US-China trade tensions; the NATO withdrawal from Afghanistan; the COVID-19 pandemic; and a 2019 Supreme Court verdict on a religious dispute site in his native India.

item.title: Trump orders tightening of defense supply-chain waiver rules
item.description: President Donald Trump on Monday signed an executive order making it harder for U.S. defense contractors to obtain waivers allowing them to buy critical minerals and other materials from China and other prohibited foreign suppliers, the administration's latest effort to reduce reliance ​on overseas supply chains for weapons production.
item.content:encoded:
• Contractors seeking waivers must show alternative searches and disclose material origins
  • The order tells the Pentagon to map lower-tier suppliers across the ​defense industrial base
  • Contractors must assess suppliers for foreign ownership financial vulnerability and manufacturing risks

Jumpstart your morning with the latest legal news delivered straight to your inbox from The Daily Docket newsletter. Sign up here.

PENTAGON PUSHES SUPPLY-CHAIN OVERHAUL

MAPPING THE INDUSTRIAL BASE

Our Standards: The Thomson Reuters Trust Principles., opens new tab

item.title: Darline Graham to run for full term to replace her brother in US Senate
item.description: U.S. Senator Darline Graham said on Monday she plans to run for a full ​term to replace her brother, late Senator Lindsey Graham, who died this ‌month after serving two decades in the U.S. Senate.
item.content:encoded:
• United States Senate
  • US Senate

The Reuters Daily Briefing newsletter provides all the news you need to start your day. Sign up here.

Our Standards: The Thomson Reuters Trust Principles., opens new tab

Jasper Ward is a breaking news reporter in Washington. She primarily covers national affairs and U.S. politics. Jasper was previously based in The Bahamas where she covered the collapse of FTX and the subsequent arrest of its founder Sam Bankman-Fried. She was a part of the Reuters team that won the Gerald Loeb Award for breaking news for its FTX coverage.

Kanishka Singh is a breaking news reporter for Reuters in Washington DC, who primarily covers US politics and national affairs in his current role. His past breaking news coverage has spanned across a range of topics like the Black Lives Matter movement; the US elections; the 2021 Capitol riots and their follow up probes; the Brexit deal; US-China trade tensions; the NATO withdrawal from Afghanistan; the COVID-19 pandemic; and a 2019 Supreme Court verdict on a religious dispute site in his native India.

item.title: Netanyahu will not be arrested in US, Trump says, as New York mayor mulls options
item.description: U.S. President Donald Trump said on Monday that Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu will not be arrested when he visits the U.S., after New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani said he was still considering ​the move.
item.content:encoded:
• Mamdani said New York City's legal department is actively considering what the law allows
  • The ICC issued a 2024 arrest warrant for ​Netanyahu over alleged Gaza war crimes
  • The United States and Israel are not members of ‌the Hague-based court

The Reuters Daily Briefing newsletter provides all the news you need to start your day. Sign up here.

ISRAEL SAYS ICC ARREST WARRANT IS 'BOGUS'

Our Standards: The Thomson Reuters Trust Principles., opens new tab

item.title: US senators at airshow to reassure foreign defense contractors, urge tariff-free trade
item.description: A bipartisan group of nine U.S. senators on Monday sought to reassure defense contractors from American allies that growing demand for ​their products is real and urged the Trump administration not to impose tariffs ‌on aerospace products.
item.content:encoded:
The Reuters Daily Briefing newsletter provides all the news you need to start your day. Sign up here.

Our Standards: The Thomson Reuters Trust Principles., opens new tab

item.title: Head of US AI safety agency resigns
item.description: U.S. Center for AI Standards and Innovation Director Chris Fall has resigned just ​three months after being appointed to lead the federal ‌AI testing institute at the Department of Commerce, a department spokesperson told Reuters, marking the latest shakeup in the Trump administration's plans on AI.
item.content:encoded:
• Alphabet Inc
  • Microsoft Corp

The Reuters Daily Briefing newsletter provides all the news you need to start your day. Sign up here.

Our Standards: The Thomson Reuters Trust Principles., opens new tab

Courtney Rozen reports on the world's largest technology companies from Washington, D.C., focusing on the relationship between the tech industry and the U.S. government. She reported on DOGE and the federal workforce during the first year of U.S. President Donald Trump’s second term. Prior to joining Reuters, she was a White House correspondent at Bloomberg Government. She graduated from American University with a master's degree in journalism. Contact: courtney.rozen@thomsonreuters.com

item.title: US FDA continues investigating Taylor Farms as cyclosporiasis cases mount
item.description: The U.S. FDA on Monday said the basis for linking Taylor Farms' ‌lettuce from Central Mexico to the largest U.S. foodborne illness outbreak in recent years remains unchanged, defending its decision to publicize a sample that a later review found was a false positive for the cyclospora parasite.
item.content:encoded:
• FDA says false-positive doesn't affect outbreak conclusions
  • Outbreak linked to Taco Bell spans 1,600+ cases in five states
  • Taco Bell consumer traffic dropped 18.9%

Keep up with the latest medical breakthroughs and healthcare trends with the Reuters Health Rounds newsletter. Sign up here.

GOTTLIEB SAYS INVESTIGATION MATTERS MORE

Our Standards: The Thomson Reuters Trust Principles., opens new tab

Washington-based correspondent covering U.S. healthcare and pharmaceutical policy with a focus on the Department of Health and Human Services and the agencies it oversees such as the Food and Drug Administration, previously based in Iraq and Egypt.

item.title: New York army veteran in custody after fire set outside ICE offices
item.description: Authorities said a man protesting Immigration and Customs Enforcement was taken into ​custody on Monday after he ignited a fire and fireworks on the ‌steps of the building that houses the agency's office in Lower Manhattan.
item.content:encoded:
The Reuters Daily Briefing newsletter provides all the news you need to start your day. Sign up here.

Our Standards: The Thomson Reuters Trust Principles., opens new tab

Maria Tsvetkova is an investigative journalist originally from Moscow who focuses on Russia’s invasion of Ukraine as part of an international team of reporters. She has reported from the conflict zones in Ukraine and Nagorno-Karabakh, as well as doing reporting stints in Armenia, Turkey and Iraq.

item.title: Senator says US daylight saving time bill faces tough fight
item.description: A bill passed by the U.S. House of Representatives last week to make daylight saving ​time permanent will face a tough fight in the Senate to win approval, ‌a lawmaker said Monday.
item.content:encoded:
• U.S. House of Representatives

The Reuters Daily Briefing newsletter provides all the news you need to start your day. Sign up here.

item.title: Meta faces Tennessee trial over allegations Instagram was designed to be addictive
item.description: Meta Platforms faces trial in Tennessee on Monday over the state's claims that Instagram's design is to blame for a youth mental-health ​crisis, one of several trials in the coming weeks testing allegations that the company's social media platforms were intentionally built to be addictive.
item.content:encoded:
• Jury selection begins Monday in Nashville for a seven-week trial
  • Tennessee seeks penalties and an order requiring Instagram platform changes
  • A New Mexico jury awarded that state $375 million in damages earlier this year

Jumpstart your morning with the latest legal news delivered straight to your inbox from The Daily Docket newsletter. Sign up here.

Our Standards: The Thomson Reuters Trust Principles., opens new tab

Diana reports on product liability, litigation, mass torts and the plaintiffs' bar. She previously worked at Law360 and the Chicago Sun-Times.

item.title: Trump calls for review related to scientific manual used by judges
item.description: U.S. President Donald Trump called for a review related to the Federal Judicial Center's ​scientific reference manual in cases involving climate change, writing ‌in a Truth Social post on Sunday that the work contains "discredited" information that led to "huge losses" for the country.
item.content:encoded:
Jumpstart your morning with the latest legal news delivered straight to your inbox from The Daily Docket newsletter. Sign up here.

item.title: Boos as Trump and Infantino enter the field for World Cup trophy ceremony
item.description: Boos descended from the stands as ​U.S. President Donald Trump and FIFA ‌chief Gianni Infantino took to the field for the World Cup trophy ceremony at New ​York New Jersey stadium on Sunday.
item.content:encoded:
• Infantino, Llc

The Reuters Daily Briefing newsletter provides all the news you need to start your day. Sign up here.

Our Standards: The Thomson Reuters Trust Principles., opens new tab

item.title: US House defeats bid to cut off Israel aid in vote dividing Democrats
item.description:
Reporting by Patricia Zengerle and Richard Cowan; Editing by Rod Nickel

Our Standards: The Thomson Reuters Trust Principles., opens new tab
item.content:encoded:
Reporting by Patricia Zengerle and Richard Cowan; Editing by Rod Nickel

Our Standards: The Thomson Reuters Trust Principles., opens new tab

Purchase Licensing Rights
Patricia Zengerle

Thomson Reuters

Patricia Zengerle has reported from more than 20 countries, including Afghanistan, Iraq, Pakistan, Saudi Arabia and China. An award-winning Washington-based national security and foreign policy reporter who also has worked as an editor, Patricia has appeared on NPR, C-Span and other programs, spoken at the National Press Club and attended the Hoover Institution Media Roundtable. She is a recipient of the Edwin M. Hood Award for Diplomatic Correspondence.

    World

    • Fury Gold suspends Quebec drilling after forest fire evacuation

      Worldcategory · July 15, 2026 · 5:50 PM EDT · 18 mins ago

      Fury Gold Mines said on Wednesday it ​has temporarily suspended exploration ‌and drilling at its Eau Claire project in northern ​Quebec after evacuating ​all personnel due to a ⁠nearby forest fire.

World

  • England tried to hold on but it wasn't enough, says Kane

    Sportscategory · July 15, 2026 · 5:49 PM EDT · 19 mins ago

    England's Harry Kane ‌was gutted after his side conceded two late goals to lose to Argentina in the World Cup semi-final on Wednesday, and the captain said they tried to hold on after going ahead but it ​was just not enough.

item.title: Civil rights leaders announce 'March on Washington' to defend voting rights
item.description:
Bianca Flowers in Chicago; additional reporting by Susan Heavey in Washington; Editing by Kat Stafford, Edmund Klamann and Aurora Ellis

Our Standards: The Thomson Reuters Trust Principles., opens new tab
item.content:encoded:
Bianca Flowers in Chicago; additional reporting by Susan Heavey in Washington; Editing by Kat Stafford, Edmund Klamann and Aurora Ellis

Our Standards: The Thomson Reuters Trust Principles., opens new tab

  • Human Rights
  • Worker Rights
    Purchase Licensing Rights
    Bianca Flowers

    Thomson Reuters

    Bianca Flowers is an award-winning multimedia journalist based in Chicago where she focuses on enterprise stories in areas of race, inequality, identity and social justice. She joined Reuters in 2022 as a manufacturing correspondent, covering the bedrock of the U.S. economy. She reported on labor unions strikes, corporate finance for global agriculture and construction companies and the impact of automation and artificial intelligence in the industrial sector. Prior to joining Reuters, she was a Senior Video Journalist at Dow Jones, covering short and long-form features on personal finance, income inequality, and diversity in the tech industry.

    World

    • Argentine players brandish political Falklands flag after England match

      Sportscategory · July 15, 2026 · 5:57 PM EDT · 12 mins ago

      Argentina players held up a political banner declaring "Las Malvinas Son Argentinas" ("The Falklands are Argentine") after their 2-1 World ​Cup semi-final victory over England on Wednesday, in apparent ‌contravention of FIFA rules.
